questions.question:
Based on the given statements, select the appropriate option with respect to grammar and usage.

Statements:
(i) The height of Mr. X is 6 feet.
(ii) The height of Mr. Y is 5 feet.

A. Mr. X is longer than Mr. Y
B. Mr. X is more elongated than Mr. Y
C. Mr. X is taller than Mr. Y
D. Mr. X is lengthier than Mr. Y
questions.answer: C. Mr. X is taller than Mr. Y
questions.to_find: grammatically and contextually correct comparative sentence for comparing heights of two people

understand.plain: When comparing the heights of people, English grammar requires using 'taller' as the comparative adjective, not 'longer', 'elongated', or 'lengthier'. 'Taller' is the standard and correct word used for comparing human height. Options A, B, and D use words that are either incorrect in register or grammatically improper when applied to human beings.

keywords.explain: A form of an adjective used to compare two things, typically formed by adding '-er' or using 'more'.
keywords.example: 'taller' is the comparative of 'tall'; 'more beautiful' is the comparative of 'beautiful'.

keywords.explain: The correct comparative adjective for 'tall', used specifically when referring to the height of people or upright objects.
keywords.example: Mr. X is taller than Mr. Y because he is 6 feet while Mr. Y is only 5 feet.

keywords.explain: The level of formality and appropriateness of a word in a given context; some words are technically comparative but not appropriate for human height.
keywords.example: 'Longer' is used for objects like ropes or rods, not for human height comparisons.

understand.visual_alt: Two men of different heights with Mr. X at 6 feet and Mr. Y at 5 feet shown side by side.

given.aim: Select the grammatically correct sentence comparing the heights of Mr. X (6 feet) and Mr. Y (5 feet)

terms.example: Mr. X stands at 6 feet tall.
terms.connects: X is taller, so we need a comparative form of 'tall'.

terms.example: Mr. Y stands at 5 feet tall.
terms.connects: Y is the shorter person, so X is 'taller than' Y.

terms.example: She is taller than her brother.
terms.connects: The word 'tall' and its comparative 'taller' are the standard terms for human height.

given.plan: Identify which option uses the grammatically correct and contextually appropriate comparative adjective for comparing human heights. 'Tall' -> 'taller' is the right choice; 'longer', 'more elongated', and 'lengthier' are inappropriate for people.

steps.title: Determine what is being compared
steps.apply: We are comparing heights of two people. The correct adjective for human height is 'tall', not 'long', 'elongated', or 'lengthy'.
steps.note: 'Long', 'elongated', and 'lengthy' are used for objects, not people's height.

steps.title: Form the correct comparative
steps.apply: Comparative of 'tall' is 'taller'. Since Mr. X (6 ft) > Mr. Y (5 ft), the sentence is: 'Mr. X is taller than Mr. Y.' This matches Option C.
steps.note: Option D ('lengthier') is doubly wrong: wrong word and 'more lengthier' would be a double comparative error.

solution.result: C. Mr. X is taller than Mr. Y

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests knowledge of English comparative adjectives for human height.

questions.question:
An apple costs Rs. 10. An onion costs Rs. 8.

Select the most suitable sentence with respect to grammar and usage.

A. The price of an apple is greater than an onion.
B. The price of an apple is more than onion.
C. The price of an apple is greater than that of an onion.
D. Apples are more costlier than onions.
questions.answer: C. The price of an apple is greater than that of an onion.
questions.to_find: grammatically correct sentence comparing the prices of an apple and an onion

understand.plain: When comparing two nouns using a quality of one (like 'price'), you must use a pronoun ('that') to refer back to the same quality of the second noun to avoid an illogical comparison. Saying 'the price of an apple is greater than an onion' compares a price to a fruit, which is illogical. Option D has a double comparative error ('more costlier').

keywords.explain: In grammar, when comparing two things, you must compare like with like — a price must be compared to a price, not to an object.
keywords.example: Correct: 'The salary of a doctor is higher than that of a nurse.' Incorrect: 'The salary of a doctor is higher than a nurse.'

keywords.explain: A pronoun phrase used to avoid repeating a noun; 'that of an onion' stands for 'the price of an onion'.
keywords.example: The speed of light is greater than that of sound.

keywords.explain: An error where two comparative markers are used together, e.g., 'more taller' or 'more costlier', which is grammatically incorrect.
keywords.example: 'More costlier' is wrong; the correct form is simply 'costlier' or 'more costly'.

understand.visual_alt: An apple priced at Rs. 10 and an onion priced at Rs. 8 shown side by side for price comparison.

given.aim: Identify the grammatically correct sentence that properly compares the price of an apple with that of an onion

terms.connects: We are comparing this price to the onion's price, so we need 'that of' to refer back.
'That of an onion' replaces 'price of an onion' to avoid an illogical noun comparison.

terms.meaning: Compare the same category of things (price with price, not price with fruit)
terms.example: The weight of iron is greater than that of cotton. (not: greater than cotton)
terms.connects: This rule makes Option C the only grammatically sound choice.

given.plan: Check each option for two errors: (1) illogical comparison (price vs. fruit) and (2) double comparative. Option C correctly uses 'that of an onion' to compare price with price.

steps.title: Eliminate options with illogical comparisons
steps.apply: Option A: 'price of apple > an onion' compares a price to a fruit — illogical. Option B: 'price of apple > onion' has the same flaw and also drops the article. Both are eliminated.
steps.note: The compared items must belong to the same category for the sentence to be logically sound.

steps.title: Eliminate Option D for double comparative error
steps.apply: 'More costlier' is a double comparative — 'more' and '-er' cannot be used together. 'Costlier' or 'more costly' would each be correct alone, but not combined.
steps.note: Double comparatives are a classic grammar error tested in GATE verbal aptitude sections.

steps.title: Confirm Option C
steps.apply: Option C: 'The price of an apple is greater than that of an onion.' Here 'that of an onion' = 'the price of an onion', making it a logical price-to-price comparison with correct grammar.
steps.note: This is the standard rule: use 'that of' or 'those of' to maintain parallel comparison.

solution.result: C. The price of an apple is greater than that of an onion.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests the grammar rule of logical parallel comparison using 'that of'.

questions.question:
It is ___________ to read this year's textbook ________ the last year's.

A. easier, than
B. most easy, than
C. easier, from
D. most easiest, than
questions.to_find: correct pair of comparative adjective and preposition to complete the sentence

understand.plain: The sentence compares two things (this year's textbook vs. last year's textbook), so a comparative adjective is needed, not a superlative. The correct comparative of 'easy' is 'easier', and the standard preposition used with comparatives is 'than'. 'From' is incorrect with comparatives, and 'most easiest' is a double superlative error.

keywords.explain: Comparative forms compare two items (-er / more); superlative forms rank among three or more (-est / most).
keywords.example: easier (comparative of easy) vs. easiest (superlative of easy).

keywords.explain: The preposition that follows a comparative adjective to introduce the second element of comparison.
keywords.example: This book is heavier than that one.

keywords.explain: Using both 'most' and '-est' together is a grammatical error, as in 'most easiest'.
keywords.example: 'Most easiest' is wrong; the correct superlative is simply 'easiest'.

understand.visual_alt: Two textbooks side by side labelled this year and last year to illustrate a two-item comparison.

given.aim: Fill in the blanks with the correct comparative adjective and the correct preposition

terms.connects: Since only two items are being compared, the comparative form 'easier' (not superlative 'easiest') is required.

terms.meaning: The word connecting the two items in a comparison
terms.connects: 'Than' is always used after a comparative adjective; 'from' is used for origin or difference, not comparison.

terms.meaning: 'easier' — formed by adding '-er' since 'easy' is a two-syllable word ending in 'y'
terms.example: This puzzle is easier than the previous one.
terms.connects: The adjective 'easy' drops the 'y' and adds '-ier' to form 'easier'.

given.plan: Identify that only two items are compared (requiring comparative, not superlative), then pair 'easier' with 'than' as the grammatically correct fill.

steps.title: Identify the type of comparison
steps.apply: The sentence compares this year's textbook with last year's — exactly two items. This calls for a comparative adjective, not a superlative.
steps.note: Superlative is only used when ranking among three or more items.

steps.title: Form the correct comparative of 'easy'
steps.apply: Comparative of 'easy' = 'easier' (drop 'y', add '-ier'). Options B ('most easy') and D ('most easiest') use superlative or double superlative — both wrong for a two-item comparison.
steps.note: 'Most easy' is also non-standard; the correct superlative is 'easiest', but even that is wrong here.

steps.title: Select the correct preposition
steps.apply: Comparative adjectives are followed by 'than'. Option C uses 'from', which is incorrect in this context. Option A uses 'than', which is correct.
steps.note: The complete correct sentence: 'It is easier to read this year's textbook than the last year's.'

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests comparative adjective formation and the correct preposition 'than' after a comparative.

questions.question:
We __________ our friend's birthday and we __________ how to make it up to him.

A. completely forgot --- don't just know
B. forgot completely --- don't just know
C. completely forgot --- just don't know
D. forgot completely --- just don't know
questions.answer: D. forgot completely --- just don't know
questions.to_find: correct placement of adverbs 'completely' and 'just' relative to verbs in both blanks

understand.plain: In English, adverbs of manner or degree like 'completely' typically follow the verb they modify when it is a simple past form without an object, making 'forgot completely' the natural order. Similarly, 'just' as a limiting adverb placed before 'don't know' produces an unnatural emphasis; the natural phrasing is 'just don't know', but the debate is on where 'completely' falls relative to 'forgot'. The standard spoken and written English usage prefers 'forgot completely' and 'just don't know'.

keywords.explain: Adverbs can modify verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs. Their position in a sentence affects meaning and naturalness.
keywords.example: 'He completely forgot' and 'He forgot completely' are both acceptable, but the latter is more natural with simple past intransitive verbs.

keywords.explain: An adverb like 'just' that limits or restricts meaning. It usually precedes the word it modifies.
keywords.example: 'I just don't know' means 'I simply do not know', placing 'just' before 'don't know' is standard.

keywords.explain: Adverbs of completeness (like 'completely') can appear at the end of the verb phrase, especially in simple past.
keywords.example: 'We forgot completely' places the adverb at the end, emphasising the total nature of forgetting.

understand.visual_alt: Two blank slots in a sentence about forgetting a birthday and not knowing how to make amends.

given.aim: Fill both blanks with the correct adverb order for 'completely/forgot' and 'just/don't know'

terms.example: 'forgot completely' — adverb placed after the intransitive verb
terms.connects: End-position placement of 'completely' after 'forgot' is more natural and standard.

terms.example: 'just don't know' — 'just' precedes the negative auxiliary 'don't'
terms.connects: 'Just' as a limiting adverb naturally precedes the verb phrase it restricts.

terms.meaning: Grammarians accept both mid and end position for 'completely', but 'forgot completely' better emphasises complete forgetting
terms.example: Compare: 'I completely forgot' (mid) vs. 'I forgot completely' (end) — both valid, latter more emphatic
terms.connects: Official answer D prefers 'forgot completely --- just don't know'.

given.plan: For blank 1, decide whether 'completely' precedes or follows 'forgot'. For blank 2, decide whether 'just' precedes 'don't know'. Check naturalness and official answer alignment.

steps.title: Analyse blank 1: 'completely' placement
steps.apply: Both 'completely forgot' and 'forgot completely' are grammatically acceptable. However, 'forgot completely' places emphasis at the end (end-weight principle) and sounds more natural in informal spoken English. Options A and C use 'completely forgot'; Options B and D use 'forgot completely'.
steps.note: The official GATE answer selects 'forgot completely', aligning with natural end-position adverb usage.

steps.title: Analyse blank 2: 'just' placement
steps.apply: 'Just don't know' is the standard phrasing — 'just' (limiting adverb) precedes the auxiliary 'don't'. 'Don't just know' changes meaning to 'not merely knowing', which is illogical in context. Options A and B use 'don't just know'; Options C and D use 'just don't know'. Only D satisfies both blanks.
steps.note: 'Don't just know' would imply knowing is not sufficient, which distorts the intended meaning of uncertainty.

solution.result: D. forgot completely --- just don't know

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests adverb placement rules in English grammar.

questions.question:
The Tamil version of __________ John Abraham-starrer Madras Cafe __________ cleared by the Censor Board with no cuts last week, but the film's distributor __________ no takers among the exhibitors for a release in Tamilnadu __________ this Friday.

A. Mr., was, found, on
B. a, was, found, at
C. the, was, found, on
D. a, is, found, at
questions.to_find: correct articles and verb form to fill the four blanks in the passage

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of English articles and tense consistency. 'John Abraham-starrer Madras Cafe' is a specific, well-known film, so the definite article 'the' is correct for blank 1. The events (clearing by Censor Board, finding no takers) happened last week, so the simple past tense 'was' is required for blank 2. 'Found' is correct for blank 3 as the distributor found (discovered) no takers. 'On' is the correct preposition before a day/date ('on Friday') for blank 4.

keywords.explain: Used before a specific or unique noun that both speaker and listener can identify.
keywords.example: 'The Tamil version of the Madras Cafe' — referring to a specific, well-known film.

keywords.explain: Used for completed actions in the past. Since the clearing happened 'last week', past tense 'was' is needed.
keywords.example: 'The film was cleared last week' describes a completed past event.

keywords.explain: In English, 'on' is used with specific days ('on Friday', 'on Monday') while 'at' is used with times ('at 5 pm') and 'in' with months/years.
keywords.example: 'The release was scheduled on this Friday' is correct; 'at this Friday' is incorrect.

understand.visual_alt: A fill-in-the-blanks passage about the Tamil release of Madras Cafe with four gaps for articles, verb, and preposition.

given.aim: Fill all four blanks with the correct article, auxiliary verb, main verb, and preposition

terms.term: blank 1 — article before 'John Abraham-starrer Madras Cafe'
terms.meaning: Definite article 'the' because Madras Cafe is a specific known film
terms.example: The Tamil version of the widely discussed film
terms.connects: 'The' specifies a known entity; 'a' would suggest an unspecified film, which is wrong here.

terms.meaning: 'was' — simple past passive, consistent with 'last week' time reference
terms.example: The film was cleared last week.
terms.connects: 'Is' (present) contradicts 'last week'; 'was' is correct for past completed action.

terms.example: The distributor found no takers among the exhibitors.
terms.connects: 'Found' (simple past of 'find') is the correct verb meaning 'encountered' or 'discovered'.

terms.meaning: 'on' — correct preposition before a specific day ('this Friday')
terms.example: The premiere is scheduled on Friday.
terms.connects: 'At' is used with clock times, not with day names; 'on' is mandatory before 'this Friday'.

given.plan: Evaluate each blank independently: blank 1 needs the definite article 'the' (specific film); blank 2 needs past tense 'was' (last week); blank 3 needs 'found' (discovered); blank 4 needs 'on' (preposition before a day name).

steps.title: Choose the article for blank 1
steps.apply: Madras Cafe is a specific known Bollywood film. The definite article 'the' is correct. Options A (Mr.) is nonsensical. Options B and D use 'a' (indefinite), which is wrong for a named specific film. Only C uses 'the'.
steps.note: 'Mr.' makes no grammatical sense before a film title in this context.

steps.title: Determine tense for blank 2
steps.apply: The sentence says 'last week', confirming past time. 'Was' (simple past passive auxiliary) is required. Option D uses 'is' (present tense), which contradicts 'last week'. Options B and C both use 'was', but B has 'a' for blank 1.
steps.note: Tense must be consistent with time expressions in the sentence.

steps.title: Confirm blanks 3 and 4
steps.apply: Blank 3: 'found' is correct — the distributor found (discovered/encountered) no takers. Blank 4: 'on this Friday' is correct preposition usage for a day. Option C gives: the, was, found, on — all four blanks correct.
steps.note: 'On' is the required preposition before days of the week in English.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests English article usage, tense selection, and prepositional correctness.

questions.question:
Research in the workplace reveals that people work for many reasons _______________.

A. money beside
B. beside money
C. money besides
D. besides money
questions.to_find: correct word and word order to complete the sentence about reasons people work

understand.plain: The sentence needs a phrase meaning 'in addition to money' or 'other than money'. The word 'besides' (a preposition/adverb meaning 'in addition to') is correct here, not 'beside' (which means 'next to' physically). The phrase 'besides money' must come at the end of the sentence with 'besides' preceding its object 'money'. Placing 'money' before 'besides' reverses the correct order.

keywords.explain: 'Besides' means 'in addition to' or 'apart from'. It is used to add extra information.
keywords.example: 'Besides money, people work for job satisfaction.' means 'In addition to money...'

keywords.explain: 'Beside' means 'next to' or 'by the side of'. It describes physical proximity, not addition.
keywords.example: 'She sat beside her friend' means she sat next to her friend.

keywords.explain: In English, a preposition must directly precede its object noun. 'Besides money' is correct; 'money besides' reverses this and is grammatically incorrect.
keywords.example: 'Besides salary, there are bonuses.' — preposition 'besides' precedes its object 'salary'.

understand.visual_alt: A workplace scene with a person working, illustrating motivations beyond just monetary compensation.

given.aim: Select the phrase that correctly completes the sentence, meaning 'in addition to money'

terms.meaning: 'beside' = next to (physical location); 'besides' = in addition to (inclusion/addition)
terms.example: He sat beside me. (location) | Besides working hard, he is talented. (addition)
terms.connects: The sentence needs 'besides' (addition), not 'beside' (location). Options A and B with 'beside' are eliminated.

terms.meaning: The preposition 'besides' must come before its noun object 'money'
terms.example: 'Besides money' is correct; 'money besides' is a broken phrase with wrong order.
terms.connects: Option C ('money besides') has the words reversed, making it grammatically wrong.

terms.meaning: The sentence states people work for many reasons; the blank adds 'money' as one such reason
terms.example: People work for many reasons besides money — meaning money is not the only motivation.
terms.connects: Option D 'besides money' correctly adds money as one of many reasons, completing the sentence naturally.

given.plan: First eliminate options using 'beside' (wrong meaning — physical proximity). Then eliminate 'money besides' (wrong word order). The correct answer is 'besides money'.

steps.title: Distinguish 'beside' from 'besides'
steps.apply: 'Beside' = next to (location). 'Besides' = in addition to (addition). The sentence talks about motivations for working, not physical location, so 'beside' (Options A, B) is wrong. Eliminate A and B.
steps.note: This is one of the most commonly confused word pairs in English grammar tests.

steps.title: Check word order in remaining options
steps.apply: Option C: 'money besides' — this places the noun before the preposition, which is incorrect. A preposition must precede its object. Option D: 'besides money' — preposition 'besides' correctly precedes its object 'money'.
steps.note: The completed sentence reads: 'Research in the workplace reveals that people work for many reasons besides money.' — grammatically correct and meaningful.

questions.formula_note: No mathematical formulas apply; this question tests the distinction between 'beside' (next to) and 'besides' (in addition to), plus correct preposition-object order.
